Apple___Apple_scab.Cause: Fungus that spreads in cool, wet weather. Spores from fallen leaves infect new growth in spring.
Apple___Apple_scab.Recommendation:
- Plant resistant varieties like 'Liberty'.
- Space trees for good air flow.
- Avoid watering leaves.
- Rake and destroy fallen leaves in autumn.
- Apply dormant oil spray in early spring.
Apple___Apple_scab.Treatment: Apply fungicide from green tip stage to petal fall, every 7–14 days during wet periods. Use products with myclobutanil, captan, or sulfur. For organic, use copper or potassium bicarbonate.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper-based fungicides, potassium bicarbonate, sulfur, neem oil, Bacillus subtilis.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Myclobutanil, Captan, Mancozeb, Fenbuconazole, Trifloxystrobin.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Microsphaeropsis ochracea, Aureobasidium pullulans (suppresses spore germination).

Apple___Black_rot.Cause: Fungus that lives in dead wood and old fruit. Spreads by rain and wind, entering through wounds.
Apple___Black_rot.Recommendation:
- Prune out and destroy infected branches.
- Remove old fruit from tree and ground.
- Avoid injuring bark.
- Keep tree healthy with good fertilizer.
- Let sunlight into canopy by pruning.
Apple___Black_rot.Treatment: Apply protective fungicide at pink bud stage and every 10–14 days during wet weather. Use captan, thiophanate-methyl, or myclobutanil. Remove infected fruit quickly.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper-based fungicides, Bordeaux mixture, sulfur, potassium bicarbonate.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Captan, Thiophanate-methyl, Myclobutanil, Pyraclostrobin, Tebuconazole, Mancozeb.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Trichoderma harzianum (on wounds), Bacillus subtilis.

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.Cause: Fungus that needs both apple trees and red cedar trees to complete its life cycle. Spores from cedar galls infect apple leaves in spring during rainy weather.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.Recommendation:
- Remove nearby red cedars if possible.
- Plant resistant varieties.
- Apply preventive fungicide before infection periods.
- Improve air circulation.
- Check cedar trees for galls in spring.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.Treatment: Apply fungicide at pink bud stage, repeat every 7–10 days until petal fall. Use myclobutanil, fenarimol, or trifloxystrobin. Organic options: sulfur or copper.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Wettable sulfur, copper hydroxide, Bordeaux mixture, neem oil.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Myclobutanil, Fenarimol, Trifloxystrobin, Tebuconazole, Triadimefon, Pyraclostrobin.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus pumilus (QST 2808), Bacillus subtilis.

Apple___healthy.Cause: No disease found. Tree is healthy.
Apple___healthy.Recommendation:
- Continue good care.
- Monitor regularly for early signs.
- Use balanced fertilizer.
- Water properly.
- Keep records.
Apple___healthy.Treatment: No treatment needed. Focus on prevention: proper pruning, soil care, and regular monitoring.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Neem oil, dormant oil, compost tea, seaweed extract.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: No treatment needed. Low-dose Captan or Mancozeb only if high risk.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Mycorrhizae, Trichoderma, compost extracts.

Blueberry___healthy.Recommendation:
- Keep soil pH 4.5–5.5.
- Use blueberry fertilizer.
- Keep soil moist, especially during fruiting.
- Mulch with pine bark or sawdust.
- Prune old wood annually.
Blueberry___healthy.Treatment: No treatment needed. Maintain good cultural practices.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Neem oil, insecticidal soap, copper (late dormant), sulfur (for pH).
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: No treatment needed. Low-dose chlorothalonil only in high risk.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis (foliar), Trichoderma (root).

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.Cause: Fungus that thrives in warm, dry days with humid nights. Spreads by wind. Dense leaves make it worse.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.Recommendation:
- Plant resistant varieties if possible.
- Prune to increase air flow.
- Avoid too much nitrogen fertilizer.
- Remove badly infected shoots.
- Plant in sunny spots.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.Treatment: At first white spots, spray horticultural oil, potassium bicarbonate, or sulfur. Chemical options: myclobutanil or triforine. Repeat every 7–14 days.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Sulfur, potassium bicarbonate, neem oil, horticultural oil, copper soap.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Myclobutanil, Triforine, Tebuconazole, Fenbuconazole, Propiconazole, Metrafenone.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis, Bacillus amyloliquefaciens, Ampelomyces quisqualis.

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.Recommendation:
- Prune properly each year.
- Watch for fruit flies.
- Keep soil moisture steady.
- Apply balanced fertilizer in early spring.
- Protect blossoms from late frost.
Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.Treatment: No treatment needed. Follow routine preventive spray program.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Neem oil, horticultural oil, copper (dormant), sulfur (late dormant).
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: No treatment needed. Low-dose captan or chlorothalonil in high-risk areas.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis (monthly), compost tea, mycorrhizae.

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.Cause: Fungus that lives in old corn residue. Spreads by wind and rain splash. Favored by warm, humid weather. Worse in continuous corn fields.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.Recommendation:
- Plant resistant hybrids.
- Rotate with soybeans or other non-grass crops.
- Bury crop residue by tilling.
- Avoid too-dense planting.
- Use balanced fertilizer.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.Treatment: If disease pressure is high, apply fungicide at tasseling stage. Use azoxystrobin, pyraclostrobin, or propiconazole. Treat when disease reaches ears before milk stage.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper, sulfur, potassium bicarbonate, neem oil, Bacillus subtilis.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Azoxystrobin, Pyraclostrobin, Propiconazole, Trifloxystrobin, Fluxapyroxad + Pyraclostrobin.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus mycoides, Pseudomonas chlororaphis, Trichoderma harzianum (seed treatment).

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust.Cause: Fungus that blows in from southern areas each year. Favored by cool to moderate temperatures and high humidity.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust.Recommendation:
- Plant resistant hybrids.
- Avoid late planting.
- Space plants for air movement.
- Monitor fields regularly.
- Use proper fertilizer.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust.Treatment: Usually not needed for field corn. For sweet corn or seed corn, apply azoxystrobin, pyraclostrobin, or tebuconazole at first sign of rust.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Sulfur, copper, neem oil, potassium silicate.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Azoxystrobin, Pyraclostrobin, Tebuconazole, Propiconazole, Prothioconazole, Mancozeb.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus pumilus, Bacillus subtilis, Pseudomonas fluorescens.

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.Cause: Fungus that overwinters in corn debris. Favored by cool (65–80°F) and wet weather. Causes long cigar-shaped lesions.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.Recommendation:
- Plant resistant hybrids (with Ht genes).
- Rotate with non-cereal crops.
- Bury crop residue.
- Avoid consecutive corn crops.
- Plant at recommended time.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.Treatment: Apply fungicide at tasseling stage if disease reaches 5–10% of leaves. Use strobilurin or triazole fungicides.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper hydroxide, sulfur, neem oil, potassium bicarbonate, compost tea.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Azoxystrobin, Pyraclostrobin, Trifloxystrobin, Tebuconazole, Propiconazole, Fluxapyroxad.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis (QST713), Trichoderma viride, Streptomyces lydicus.

Corn_(maize)___healthy.Recommendation:
- Continue good care.
- Monitor fields regularly.
- Maintain soil fertility.
- Use IPM practices.
- Keep records.
Corn_(maize)___healthy.Treatment: No treatment needed. Focus on prevention: rotation, residue management, good hybrid selection.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Neem cake, seaweed extract, compost tea.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: No treatment needed. Seed treatment with mefenoxam only if disease pressure known.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Mycorrhizae (Glomus intraradices), Trichoderma seed coating.

Grape___Black_rot.Cause: Fungus that overwinters in old berries and canes. Infects during warm, wet periods. Berries are susceptible until 3–5 weeks after bloom.
Grape___Black_rot.Recommendation:
- Plant resistant varieties (e.g., 'Catawba', 'Norton').
- Prune for air flow.
- Remove old fruit.
- Orient rows for sun and air.
- Avoid overhead watering.
Grape___Black_rot.Treatment: Start fungicide when shoots are 3–6 inches long. Continue every 7–14 days through 3–5 weeks after bloom. Use mancozeb, captan, or myclobutanil. Organic: sulfur or copper.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper hydroxide, sulfur, Bordeaux mixture, potassium bicarbonate.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Mancozeb, Captan, Myclobutanil, Tebuconazole, Azoxystrobin, Pyraclostrobin.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis, Trichoderma harzianum, Streptomyces lydicus.

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).Cause: Fungus that enters through pruning wounds. Develops slowly over years. Stressed vines (drought, poor nutrition, winter injury) show symptoms faster. Affects mature vines (5+ years).
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).Recommendation:
- Protect pruning wounds with sealants.
- Prune during dry weather.
- Remove and destroy badly infected vines.
- Avoid injuring trunks.
- Keep vines vigorous with good care.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).Treatment: No cure once inside wood. Surgical removal of diseased wood may help early. Apply fungicide to wounds after pruning to prevent new infections.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: No cure. Prevention: Bordeaux mixture on wounds, sodium bicarbonate, compost tea.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Thiophanate-methyl, Tebuconazole, Flusilazole, Pyraclostrobin (preventive on wounds).
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Trichoderma harzianum (applied to pruning wounds), Bacillus subtilis.

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.Cause: Fungus that overwinters in infected leaves and canes. Favored by rainy, warm, humid weather. Worse in dense plantings with poor air circulation.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.Recommendation:
- Prune to improve air flow.
- Remove infected leaves in winter.
- Use drip irrigation, not sprinklers.
- Avoid too much nitrogen.
- Keep vines balanced.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.Treatment: Apply fungicide when shoots are 6–12 inches long, repeat every 10–14 days during wet periods. Use mancozeb, captan, or copper. Cover both leaf surfaces.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper oxychloride, sulfur, Bordeaux mixture, neem oil.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Mancozeb, Captan, Azoxystrobin, Pyraclostrobin, Tebuconazole, Fenbuconazole.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis, Bacillus amyloliquefaciens, Streptomyces griseoviridis.

Grape___healthy.Recommendation:
- Continue good care.
- Monitor regularly.
- Prune correctly.
- Balanced nutrition.
- Good floor management.
Grape___healthy.Treatment: No treatment needed. Follow routine preventive spray program for your area.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Sulfur (for mildew prevention), low-dose copper, neem oil, horticultural oil.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: No treatment needed. Low-dose mancozeb or captan only in high-risk vineyards.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis (monthly), Trichoderma root zone, compost tea.

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).Cause: Bacteria spread by tiny insects called Asian citrus psyllids. Blocks nutrient flow. No cure. Usually fatal within 2–5 years.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).Recommendation:
- Plant certified disease-free trees.
- Control psyllids aggressively.
- Remove and destroy infected trees.
- Monitor regularly.
- Work with neighbors for area-wide control.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).Treatment: No cure. Remove infected trees to stop spread. For healthy trees, use systemic insecticides to kill psyllids. Nutritional sprays may help symptoms but won't cure.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: No cure. Psyllid control: neem oil, insecticidal soap, horticultural oil, kaolin clay, reflective mulch. Nutrient therapy: zinc, manganese, iron foliar sprays.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: No cure. Psyllid control: Imidacloprid, Thiamethoxam, Fenpropathrin, Cyantraniliprole, Spirotetramat. Foliar nutrients to manage symptoms.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Tamarixia radiata (parasitic wasp against psyllids), Beauveria bassiana (fungus infects psyllids), phage therapy (experimental).

Peach___Bacterial_spot.Cause: Bacteria that overwinter in cankers and buds. Spread by rain, wind, and tools. Favored by warm (70–85°F) and wet weather.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.Recommendation:
- Plant resistant varieties.
- Choose well-drained soil with good air flow.
- Avoid overhead irrigation.
- Prune in dry weather.
- Remove severely infected trees.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.Treatment: Apply copper bactericides at leaf fall and before bud swell. During growing season, use streptomycin or oxytetracycline every 7–10 days during wet periods. Start at petal fall.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper hydroxide, fixed copper, Bordeaux mixture, copper + mancozeb.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Oxytetracycline, Streptomycin (limited), Copper + mancozeb, Kasugamycin (where registered).
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis, Bacillus amyloliquefaciens, Pseudomonas fluorescens.

Peach___healthy.Recommendation:
- Prune and train properly.
- Watch for oriental fruit moth.
- Water consistently, especially during fruit growth.
- Apply dormant oil for scale.
- Thin fruit for good size.
Peach___healthy.Treatment: No treatment needed. Follow routine spray program for your region.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Dormant oil, neem oil, copper spray (dormant).
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: No treatment needed. Preventive copper + mancozeb in spring if disease history.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis (spring foliar), compost tea & mycorrhizae.

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.Cause: Bacteria from infected seeds, transplants, or equipment. Spread by splashing water. Favored by warm (75–85°F), wet weather.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.Recommendation:
- Use certified disease-free seeds and transplants.
- Rotate crops for 2–3 years.
- Avoid working in wet plants.
- Use drip irrigation.
- Remove infected debris after harvest.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.Treatment: Apply copper bactericides preventively every 7–10 days during favorable conditions. Combine copper with mancozeb for better control. Start before symptoms appear.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper hydroxide, copper + mancozeb, Bio-copper, Regalia.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Copper + mancozeb, Copper + cyprodinil + fludioxonil, Oxytetracycline, Kasugamycin (where registered), Actigard (resistance inducer).
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis, Bacillus amyloliquefaciens, Pseudomonas syringae (competitive exclusion – experimental), Streptomyces lydicus.

Pepper,_bell___healthy.Recommendation:
- Continue good care.
- Watch for pests.
- Keep soil moisture steady.
- Use balanced fertilizer.
- Support heavy fruit.
Pepper,_bell___healthy.Treatment: No treatment needed. Maintain prevention: spacing, weed control, regular monitoring.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Neem oil, low-dose copper, Regalia, compost tea.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: No treatment needed. Preventive copper + mancozeb only during wet, high-risk periods.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis (weekly), Trichoderma root inoculants, mycorrhizae.

Potato___Early_blight.Cause: Fungus that lives in plant debris and soil. Favored by warm (75–85°F) weather with alternating wet and dry periods. Older, stressed plants are more susceptible.
Potato___Early_blight.Recommendation:
- Use certified disease-free seed.
- Rotate with non-host crops for 3 years.
- Maintain adequate potassium.
- Avoid overhead irrigation.
- Destroy crop residue after harvest.
Potato___Early_blight.Treatment: Start fungicide when plants are 6–8 inches tall. Use chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or pyraclostrobin every 7–14 days. Organic: copper or Bacillus subtilis. Cover lower leaves well.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper oxychloride, potassium bicarbonate, neem oil, hydrogen peroxide.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb, Azoxystrobin, Pyraclostrobin, Boscalid, Penthiopyrad.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis, Bacillus pumilus, Trichoderma harzianum, Streptomyces lydicus.

Potato___Late_blight.Cause: Water mold (not a true fungus) that destroys plants quickly in cool (60–70°F), wet weather. Can kill a whole field in days.
Potato___Late_blight.Recommendation:
- Plant resistant varieties if available.
- Use certified disease-free seed.
- Destroy volunteer potatoes and cull piles.
- Avoid overhead irrigation.
- Scout fields often in risky weather.
Potato___Late_blight.Treatment: Apply protective fungicide before infection. Use mancozeb, chlorothalonil, or fixed copper. If disease appears, use systemic fungicides like mefenoxam or fluopicolide. Apply every 5–7 days in high-risk periods.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper hydroxide (preventive only), potassium phosphite.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Mancozeb (preventive), Chlorothalonil (preventive), Mefenoxam (systemic), Fluopicolide, Dimethomorph, Cymoxanil, Fenamidone, Cyazofamid, Mandipropamid.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis (limited), Streptomyces lydicus, Pseudomonas fluorescens, Burkholderia cepacia (with caution), phosphite biostimulants.

Potato___healthy.Recommendation:
- Continue good care.
- Monitor fields regularly.
- Maintain soil fertility and pH.
- Water properly.
- Use IPM.
Potato___healthy.Treatment: No treatment needed. Focus on prevention: crop rotation, proper hilling, resistant varieties.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Low-dose copper (preventive), neem oil, kelp extract.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: No treatment needed. Seed treatment with mancozeb or mefenoxam for early protection.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Trichoderma harzianum (seed treatment), Bacillus subtilis (foliar), Streptomyces spp.

Raspberry___healthy.Cause: No disease. Canes and leaves are healthy.
Raspberry___healthy.Recommendation:
- Prune old canes after harvest.
- Control weeds.
- Don't over-fertilize with nitrogen.
- Use trellis for air circulation.
- Remove old canes promptly.
Raspberry___healthy.Treatment: No treatment needed. Maintain good practices to prevent gray mold and spur blight.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Neem oil, sulfur (for powdery mildew), copper (dormant), lime sulfur (dormant).
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: No treatment needed. Dormant lime sulfur or copper for sanitation.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis (spring/summer foliar), mycorrhizae, compost tea.

Soybean___healthy.Recommendation:
- Continue good field management.
- Monitor for pests and diseases.
- Maintain soil fertility.
- Plant at correct date and population.
- Keep field records.
Soybean___healthy.Treatment: No treatment needed. Focus on prevention: variety selection, crop rotation, good drainage.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Neem oil, low-dose copper, seaweed extract, compost tea.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: No treatment needed. Seed treatment with mefenoxam + fludioxonil in high-risk fields.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bradyrhizobium inoculants (for nitrogen), Trichoderma seed treatment, Bacillus subtilis (foliar).

Squash___Powdery_mildew.Cause: Fungus that spreads by wind. Favored by warm (70–80°F) days and humid nights (but no free water). Worse in shade and poor air flow.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.Recommendation:
- Plant resistant varieties if available.
- Space plants for air movement.
- Avoid overhead watering.
- Remove badly infected leaves early.
- Plant in full sun.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.Treatment: At first white spots, spray horticultural oil, potassium bicarbonate, or sulfur. Chemical: myclobutanil or trifloxystrobin every 7–10 days. Organic: milk spray (1 part milk to 9 parts water) or baking soda solution.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Sulfur, potassium bicarbonate, neem oil, horticultural oil, milk spray (1:9 milk:water), baking soda solution (1 tbsp + 1 tsp soap per gallon).
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Myclobutanil, Trifloxystrobin, Tebuconazole, Metrafenone, Cyflufenamid, Flutriafol.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis, Bacillus amyloliquefaciens, Ampelomyces quisqualis, Streptomyces lydicus.

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.Cause: Fungus that overwinters in infected leaves. Spread by splashing water. Favored by cool, wet weather. Worse in dense plantings.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.Recommendation:
- Plant resistant varieties (e.g., 'Allstar', 'Jewel').
- Remove old infected leaves after harvest.
- Increase air flow by spacing.
- Use drip irrigation.
- Renovate beds annually.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.Treatment: Start fungicide at first leaf growth in spring. Use captan, thiophanate-methyl, or myclobutanil every 10–14 days during cool, wet periods. Organic: copper or bicarbonate.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper hydroxide, sulfur, potassium bicarbonate, neem oil.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Captan, Thiophanate-methyl, Myclobutanil, Pyraclostrobin, Fenbuconazole.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis, Bacillus amyloliquefaciens, Streptomyces lydicus.

Strawberry___healthy.Recommendation:
- Continue good care.
- Monitor regularly.
- Maintain renovation schedule.
- Provide winter protection in cold areas.
- Use appropriate fertilizer.
Strawberry___healthy.Treatment: No treatment needed. Maintain preventive program for common strawberry diseases.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Neem oil, low-dose copper, low-dose sulfur (for mildew prevention).
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: No treatment needed. Low-dose captan or thiophanate-methyl only during high-risk wet periods.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis (bi-weekly foliar), Trichoderma soil drench, compost tea, mycorrhizae.

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.Cause: Bacteria from infected seeds, transplants, or splashing water. Enters through wounds. Favored by warm (75–90°F), wet, humid weather.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.Recommendation:
- Use certified disease-free seeds and transplants.
- Rotate crops for 2–3 years.
- Avoid overhead irrigation.
- Space plants for air flow.
- Remove infected debris after harvest.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.Treatment: Apply copper bactericides preventively every 7–10 days when conditions favor disease. Combine copper with mancozeb for better control. Start before symptoms appear.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper hydroxide, copper + mancozeb, Regalia, Bio-Cop.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Copper + mancozeb, Copper + cyprodinil + fludioxonil, Oxytetracycline, Actigard (resistance inducer).
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis, Bacillus amyloliquefaciens, Pseudomonas fluorescens, Bacillus pumilus.

Tomato___Early_blight.Cause: Fungus that lives in plant debris and soil. Spread by wind, rain, tools. Favored by warm (75–85°F) and humid weather. Lower leaves infected first.
Tomato___Early_blight.Recommendation:
- Plant resistant varieties if available.
- Stake and prune for air flow.
- Mulch to prevent soil splash.
- Remove lower leaves as plant grows.
- Rotate crops for 3 years.
Tomato___Early_blight.Treatment: Start fungicide when first fruit clusters form. Use chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin every 7–14 days. Organic: copper, Bacillus subtilis, or potassium bicarbonate. Cover lower leaves well.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper hydroxide, potassium bicarbonate, neem oil, Bacillus subtilis, hydrogen peroxide.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb, Azoxystrobin, Pyraclostrobin, Boscalid, Difenoconazole, Tebuconazole.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis, Bacillus amyloliquefaciens, Bacillus pumilus, Trichoderma harzianum.

Tomato___Late_blight.Cause: Water mold that spreads rapidly in cool (60–70°F), wet weather. Can destroy entire planting in days. Spores travel long distances by wind.
Tomato___Late_blight.Recommendation:
- Plant resistant varieties (e.g., 'Mountain Magic', 'Defiant').
- Use disease-free transplants.
- Space plants for air flow.
- Avoid overhead irrigation.
- Remove infected plants immediately.
Tomato___Late_blight.Treatment: Apply protective fungicide before infection. Use chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or fixed copper. If disease appears, use systemic products like mefenoxam or fluopicolide every 5–7 days during high-risk periods.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper hydroxide (preventive only), potassium phosphite, Regalia (suppressive).
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Mancozeb (preventive), Chlorothalonil (preventive), Mefenoxam (systemic), Fluopicolide, Dimethomorph, Fenamidone, Cymoxanil, Cyazofamid, Mandipropamid.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis (limited preventive), Streptomyces lydicus, Pseudomonas species (experimental), phosphite biostimulants.

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.Cause: Fungus common in greenhouses and high tunnels. Favored by high humidity (>85%) and moderate temperatures (70–75°F).
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.Recommendation:
- Plant resistant varieties (with Cf genes).
- Use fans and spacing to increase air flow.
- Ventilate to reduce humidity.
- Avoid overhead watering.
- Remove infected leaves promptly.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.Treatment: Apply fungicide at first sign. Use chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper. Biological: Bacillus subtilis or Streptomyces lydicus. Cover both leaf surfaces.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper hydroxide, sulfur, potassium bicarbonate, neem oil.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb, Tebuconazole, Trifloxystrobin, Flutriafol, Myclobutanil.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis, Bacillus amyloliquefaciens, Streptomyces lydicus, Trichoderma harzianum.

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.Cause: Fungus that overwinters in plant debris. Spread by splashing water. Favored by moderate temperatures (60–80°F) and high humidity. Lower leaves infected first.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.Recommendation:
- Rotate crops for 2–3 years.
- Stake plants for air flow.
- Mulch to prevent soil splash.
- Remove lower leaves as plant grows.
- Destroy infected debris after harvest.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.Treatment: Start fungicide when first symptoms appear. Use chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper every 7–10 days. Organic: copper or Bacillus subtilis. Remove severely infected lower leaves.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper hydroxide, potassium bicarbonate, neem oil, sulfur.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb, Azoxystrobin, Pyraclostrobin, Tebuconazole, Difenoconazole.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis, Bacillus amyloliquefaciens, Streptomyces lydicus, Trichoderma harzianum.

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.Cause: Tiny spider-like pests (not a disease). Thrive in hot, dry conditions. Reproduce quickly. Damage leaves by sucking out chlorophyll.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.Recommendation:
- Check plants regularly with a hand lens.
- Keep soil moist.
- Avoid too much nitrogen fertilizer.
- Use reflective mulches.
- Encourage natural predators like predatory mites.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.Treatment: Apply miticide if mites exceed threshold. Use abamectin, bifenazate, or spiromesifen. Organic: insecticidal soap, horticultural oil, or neem oil. Cover undersides of leaves well.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Insecticidal soap, horticultural oil (neem, canola), sulfur (don't mix with oil), rosemary oil, pyrethrin.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Abamectin, Bifenazate, Spiromesifen, Fenpyroximate, Hexythiazox, Etoxazole, Chlorfenapyr.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Phytoseiulus persimilis (most effective predatory mite), Neoseiulus californicus, Amblyseius swirskii, Stethorus punctillum (lady beetle), Beauveria bassiana, Metarhizium anisopliae.

Tomato___Target_Spot.Cause: Fungus that lives in plant debris and can infect many crops. Spread by wind, rain, tools. Favored by warm (75–85°F), wet weather.
Tomato___Target_Spot.Recommendation:
- Rotate crops for 2–3 years.
- Space plants for air flow.
- Remove crop residue after harvest.
- Use drip irrigation.
- Avoid working in wet plants.
Tomato___Target_Spot.Treatment: Apply fungicide when first symptoms appear. Use chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or strobilurin every 7–14 days. Cover all plant parts well.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Copper hydroxide, potassium bicarbonate, neem oil, Bacillus subtilis.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb, Azoxystrobin, Pyraclostrobin, Boscalid + Pyraclostrobin, Tebuconazole, Fluxapyroxad + Pyraclostrobin.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis, Bacillus amyloliquefaciens, Streptomyces lydicus, Trichoderma harzianum.

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.Cause: Virus spread by tiny whiteflies. Once whitefly picks up virus, it spreads it for life. Warm weather increases whitefly activity and virus spread.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.Recommendation:
- Plant resistant varieties (with Ty genes).
- Use virus-free transplants.
- Install reflective mulches.
- Control whiteflies with insecticides or beneficial insects.
- Remove and destroy infected plants.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.Treatment: No cure for infected plants – remove them. Prevention: control whiteflies with systemic insecticides (imidacloprid, thiamethoxam) or insect growth regulators. Use yellow sticky traps.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: No cure. Whitefly control: neem oil, insecticidal soap, horticultural oil, kaolin clay, yellow sticky traps, reflective mulch.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: No cure for virus. Whitefly control: Imidacloprid (soil drench), Thiamethoxam, Dinotefuran, Spiromesifen, Pyriproxyfen, Cyantraniliprole.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Eretmocerus mundus (parasitic wasp), Encarsia formosa, Beauveria bassiana, Isaria fumosorosea, Verticillium lecanii.

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.Cause: Virus that survives on plant debris, tools, and workers' hands. Spread by touch – not by insects. Very stable.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.Recommendation:
- Plant resistant varieties (with Tm-2 genes).
- Wash hands with soap before handling plants.
- Disinfect tools with 10% bleach.
- Don't smoke near tomatoes.
- Remove infected plants.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.Treatment: No cure. Remove infected plants. Prevention: use virus-free seeds and transplants. Strict sanitation. Rotate with non-host crops for 2 years.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: No cure. Prevention: milk spray (1:10 milk:water – induces resistance), compost tea, neem oil.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: No cure. Disinfect tools with 10% bleach, 70% ethanol, or 20% powdered milk solution. Actigard (resistance inducer).
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis (limited resistance induction), Pseudomonas fluorescens (seed treatment), Trichoderma viride (seed treatment), mycorrhizae (increases tolerance).

Tomato___healthy.Cause: No disease or pests. Plants are healthy.
Tomato___healthy.Recommendation:
- Continue good care.
- Monitor regularly.
- Water and fertilize properly.
- Stake and prune for air flow.
- Keep records.
Tomato___healthy.Treatment: No treatment needed. Follow preventive program for common tomato diseases and pests in your region.

Treatment_Plan.Organic: Neem oil, low-dose copper, insecticidal soap, Regalia, compost tea, kelp extract.
Treatment_Plan.Chemical: No treatment needed. Low-dose copper or chlorothalonil only under high disease pressure in wet regions.
Treatment_Plan.Biological: Bacillus subtilis (weekly), Trichoderma harzianum (root drench), mycorrhizae, Streptomyces lydicus.
